narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic profile, including your placement in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 also falls below the average of around 3.2, signaling potential challenges in sustaining high performance. While your LSAT score of 37 is a bit stronger than the average of 36.7, and your middle family income aligns with many applicants, these weren't enough to offset the concerns.

The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your third-year decile ranking, which had the strongest pull toward predicting a higher risk of bar failure. Coming in second was your LSAT score, followed closely by your first-year decile, your family income bracket, your Asian racial background (less common than the majority White applicants at about 84%), your undergraduate GPA, your plan to attend full-time (the norm for over 90% of students), and finally your gender as male (shared by over half of applicants). Each of these factors nudged the prediction slightly toward failure risk, with your low year-three and year-one deciles standing out as the top concerns because they deviated so far from typical student performance. In comparison, most students who pass the bar have stronger decile rankings and GPAs closer to or above average, putting you at a relative disadvantage despite your solid LSAT.

Looking ahead, you can realistically improve your bar exam odds by focusing on rebuilding your academic track record—perhaps through targeted study habits, tutoring, or additional coursework to boost future grades equivalent to higher deciles. Retaking the LSAT to push your score even higher could also help, as it already showed promise but wasn't decisive enough here. Your full-time status is standard, so sticking with it while prioritizing rigorous preparation programs designed for the bar will make the biggest difference. With dedication to these steps, you could shift your profile toward the stronger averages that predict success for most passers. Keep pushing—you have strengths to build on.
